era_context: Mid-7th century CE: The First Fitna (Islamic civil war) is at its height. The Byzantine Empire under Constans II is recovering from the Arab-Byzantine wars. Tang Dynasty China under Emperor Gaozong is consolidating power. In Europe, Merovingian Frankish kingdoms are fragmenting; the Visigothic Kingdom in Iberia is in decline. This is an era of political instability and religious transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Battle of Siffin
events.description: The Battle of Siffin (July 657 CE) was fought between the forces of Caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib and Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan, the Umayyad governor of the Levant, over succession to the caliphate following the assassination of Caliph Uthman (656 CE). The battle was one of the largest and most significant of the First Fitna (Islamic civil war). Although Ali's forces initially prevailed, Muawiyah's forces raised Qurans on their spears, prompting Ali's troops to demand arbitration rather than continue fighting. This led to the Arbitration of Adhruh, which ultimately failed and resulted in the emergence of the Kharijites as a distinct faction. The battle marked a turning point in Islamic history, leading to the eventual establishment of the Umayyad Caliphate and the crystallization of the Sunni-Shia split.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled 9th century
Early Islamic historical tradition (consensus of later sources)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Siffin is well-attested in Islamic historiography and is a major event in the First Fitna. The date of July 657 CE is widely accepted, though some sources vary by a few months.

events.title: Tang Dynasty consolidation under Emperor Gaozong and Wu Zetian's rise to influence
events.description: Emperor Gaozong of Tang (r. 649–683 CE) continued to consolidate power, with increasing influence from his consort Wu Zetian (later Empress Wu, r. 690–705 CE). By 657 CE, Wu Zetian had borne Gaozong a son (the future Emperor Zhongzong) and was gaining political influence at court. This period marks the beginning of Wu Zetian's rise to power, which would eventually lead to her becoming the only female emperor in Chinese history. The consolidation of Tang power under Gaozong and Wu Zetian's influence shaped Chinese politics for decades.
key_figures: Wu Zetian (consort, later Empress, r. 690–705 CE)

sources.name: Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu), compiled 945 CE
New Book of Tang (Xin Tangshu), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: Wu Zetian's rise to influence is well-documented in Chinese historiography, though the precise dating of specific events in 657 CE is approximate. The broader pattern of her political ascendancy is confirmed.

events.title: Byzantine military campaigns under Constans II
events.description: Emperor Constans II of the Byzantine Empire (r. 641–668 CE) continued military campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean and Anatolia. By 657 CE, Constans II was attempting to stabilize Byzantine control over key territories and maintain naval superiority against Arab expansion. These campaigns were part of the broader Arab-Byzantine wars that had been ongoing since the 630s CE.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle (Chronographia), compiled early 9th century

events.certainty_note: Byzantine military campaigns are well-attested, but specific engagements in 657 CE are not precisely documented. Dating is approximate within ±5 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Siffin is one of the most significant events of the First Fitna and is well-attested in Islamic historiography. However, some modern scholars have questioned the precise details and casualty figures reported in later sources, noting that contemporary documentation is limited. The battle's significance in Islamic history is undisputed, but the exact sequence of events and their interpretation vary across sources.
historiographic_note: 657 CE is moderately well-documented for the Islamic world, with the Battle of Siffin being a major event attested in multiple later sources. However, contemporary documentation is limited; most information comes from chronicles compiled centuries after the events. Byzantine and Chinese sources are available but less detailed for this specific year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Islamic Caliphate and Tang China; other regions are nearly absent.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Siffin was a direct consequence of the assassination of Caliph Uthman and the disputed succession.
The Battle of Siffin led to the Arbitration of Adhruh in 658 CE.
The Battle of Siffin and subsequent events led to Muawiyah's establishment of the Umayyad Caliphate in 661 CE.
